title: MA: Correcting Past Errors
concept: Moving Average (MA) models forecast future values by looking at past prediction errors (residuals), helping the model smooth out unexpected shocks.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main source of input features in an Autoregression (AR) model?

options.text: Past values (lags) of the same variable we are predicting
Past prediction errors made by the model
External data sources like temperature or weather conditions

exercises.explain: Spot on! AR models look back at past values. Today we'll look at a model that looks back at past mistakes instead!

cards.title: The Baker's Shock
cards.body: Suppose a bakery usually sells 100 baguettes a day. Suddenly, a tour bus pulls up and buys 50 extra baguettes. This is a random shock. If the baker only looked at past sales (AR), they might think demand permanently doubled. Instead, they need a way to realize it was a one-time 'error' and smooth it out.

cards.title: What is Moving Average (MA)?
cards.body: The Moving Average (MA) forecasting model predicts future values by looking at the model's own past prediction errors (also called residuals or shocks). Instead of regressing on the actual values, it regresses on how far off its previous guesses were.

cards.title: Adjusting After a Wind Gust
cards.body: Imagine throwing darts. If you throw a dart and a sudden gust of wind blows it 5 inches to the left, that's an error. On your next throw, you adjust for that specific mistake (the error) to get back on target. You don't assume the target moved; you just correct for the random shock.

cards.title: Smoothing Power Demand
cards.body: An electric grid forecasts hourly power usage. A sudden heatwave causes an unexpected spike in power use (a large error in the grid's model). A Moving Average model looks at this past error and uses it to adjust the next hour's prediction, preventing the spike from throwing off the long-term forecast.

cards.title: Not a Simple Average
cards.body: In statistics, a 'Moving Average' model is NOT the same as just taking the average of the last 5 numbers (like a rolling average). Instead, the model learns special weights for past prediction errors to predict the next deviation from the baseline.

cards.title: AR vs. MA Models
cards.body: How Autoregression (AR) and Moving Average (MA) models use different inputs:

left.text:
Uses past values (lags).
e.g. "Yesterday's sales were $500, so today's will be similar."

right.text:
Uses past errors (residuals).
e.g. "Yesterday we under-predicted by $50, so let's adjust today's prediction."

exercises.prompt: What does a Moving Average (MA) model regress on to make predictions?

options.text: Past values of the variable itself

exercises.explain: Correct! The MA model uses past forecast errors to adjust and predict future values.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a Moving Average (MA) model, 'errors' refer to mistakes in data entry, not random shocks.
exercises.explain: False. Errors (or residuals) represent the difference between the model's prediction and the actual value, capturing unexpected random shocks.

exercises.sentence: While an AR model uses past observations, an MA model uses past prediction ___.
exercises.explain: MA models look at past errors to smooth out unexpected shocks and return to a baseline prediction.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following scenario designs shows an MA model at work?

options.text: Adjusting tomorrow's sales prediction based on how much the model under-estimated today's sales
Adding up all sales from the last 30 days and dividing by 30 to get a flat average
Predicting sales using the historical trend line alone

exercises.explain: Yes! Using the difference between the actual and predicted values (errors) to adjust the next step is the MA method.

exercises.prompt: A stock trading firm experiences a sudden, one-day market crash due to a computer glitch. The market recovers the next day. How would an MA model handle this compared to an AR model?

options.text: The MA model recognizes the crash as a short-lived prediction error and quickly dampens its impact, whereas an AR model might assume a downward trend and continue predicting low prices.
The MA model would treat the crash as a permanent trend, whereas the AR model would ignore it completely.
Both models would predict a complete, permanent crash for the next month.
The MA model would require recalculating the PCA components to understand the glitch.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Because the MA model regresses on past errors, it treats sudden spikes as temporary shocks (residuals) that fade out quickly, smoothing the forecast. Next, let's learn how to deal with steady upward or downward trends!
